heb.paragraph: The noun euangelion (gospel) frames the opening: Paul 'preached' it, they 'received' it, they 'stand' on it, they are 'saved' by it. The term here denotes a specific proclamation with defined content: Christ's death, burial, resurrection, and appearances (vv. 3–8). This is the earliest extant summary of the Christian kerygma, predating the written Gospels by at least a decade.

The verb egeirō in the perfect tense (egēgertai, 'has been raised,' v. 4) is theologically decisive. The perfect denotes a completed past action with ongoing present consequences: Christ was raised and remains raised. The passive implies divine agency: God raised Christ.

refs.note: 'Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures' draws on the Suffering Servant passage.
The early church interpreted this as a prophecy of Christ's resurrection (cf. Acts 2:25–31).
The Gospel resurrection narratives expand the creedal statement.

notes.note: Paul recalls the Corinthians to the foundational gospel. The conditional 'if you hold firmly' introduces pastoral urgency: some risk believing 'in vain.'

The earliest written summary of the Christian gospel. Four verbs: 'died... buried... raised... appeared.' The witness list is impressive: Cephas, the Twelve, over five hundred, James, all the apostles, and Paul.
Paul's self-designation as 'one abnormally born' reflects his awareness of an irregular calling. Yet divine grace transformed the persecutor into the most energetic apostle.

Calvin treasures vv. 3–5 as the irreducible core of the gospel. The burial proves the death was physical; the appearances prove the resurrection was physical. The appeal to five hundred witnesses is a mark of historical honesty. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note identifies vv. 3b–5 as a pre-Pauline creedal formula based on non-Pauline vocabulary, formulaic structure, and Paul's language of tradition reception.
The NET note on ektrōma ('one abnormally born') discusses whether this is Paul's self-deprecation or an opponent's insult he co-opts.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues vv. 3–7 constitute the earliest recoverable Christian creed, dating to c. AD 30–35. The phrase 'according to the Scriptures' integrates Christ's work into the larger story of God's redemptive purpose. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton argues the creedal formula shows signs of Semitic origin and was likely formulated in Aramaic before being translated into Greek. Paul's inclusion of himself simultaneously claims apostolic authority and acknowledges its irregular basis. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Chapter 15 addresses a denial among some Corinthians of future bodily resurrection. They may have held a 'realised eschatology' influenced by Greek disdain for the body. Paul's response begins with the foundational kerygma (vv. 1–11). The creedal formula in vv. 3–5 is a pre-Pauline tradition using the technical language of Jewish tradition-transmission (paralambanō/paradidōmi).

sections.header: Verses 12–19 — "If Christ Has Not Been Raised"

heb.paragraph: The noun kērygma (proclamation) in v. 14 denotes the message's content. If Christ has not been raised, the kērygma is 'useless' (kenon, empty)—a container without contents.
The adjective mataios (futile) in v. 17 describes faith without resurrection: it achieves nothing. The word echoes Ecclesiastes' mataiōtēs (vanity).

refs.note: Christ 'was raised to life for our justification'—resurrection is essential to salvation's application, not merely its confirmation.
Peter grounds Christian hope in the resurrection.

notes.note: Paul's logical chain: deny the category (resurrection of the dead) and you deny the instance (Christ's resurrection). If Christ has not been raised, the apostles are false witnesses.
Without resurrection, faith is futile, believers remain in sins, the dead have perished, and Christians are the most pitiable of all people.

Calvin notes Paul's intellectual honesty: he demonstrates the incoherence of denying bodily resurrection while affirming Christ's. The entire edifice of salvation collapses without resurrection. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note distinguishes kenon (empty of content) from mataia (failing to achieve its aim). The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee emphasises that the Corinthians' denial reflects Greek disdain for the body. Paul demonstrates that spiritual resurrection cannot be separated from bodily resurrection. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton analyses the logical structure as a classical modus tollens argument, one of the most rigorous pieces of argumentation in the Pauline corpus. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Paul constructs a reductio ad absurdum: if there is no resurrection, seven catastrophic consequences follow. The argument is logically airtight: Paul refuses to separate Christ's resurrection from the general resurrection.

heb.paragraph: The noun aparchē (firstfruits) is an agricultural-cultic metaphor. Christ's resurrection is the firstfruits: not merely the first in a series but the guarantee that the full harvest will follow. The metaphor implies organic continuity.
The military noun tagma (order) arranges the resurrection in stages: Christ first, then believers at the parousia, then the consummation when Christ hands the kingdom to the Father.

refs.note: Death as a consequence of Adam's sin provides the negative background for the Adam-Christ contrast.
The most-cited OT text in the NT, applied to Christ's present reign.
Christ's exaltation leads to universal submission and the Father's glorification.

notes.note: The triumphant 'But' of v. 20 reverses v. 19's despair. The Adam-Christ typology establishes the corporate, representative character of both death and resurrection.

The eschatological timeline: Christ first, believers at the parousia, then the consummation. The 'last enemy' is death itself. The Son's voluntary subordination to the Father consummates redemptive history.

Calvin's richest theological treatment in the chapter. The firstfruits metaphor guarantees believers' resurrection. On v. 28, the Son's 'subjection' is the consummation of the mediatorial kingdom, not a diminishment of deity. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note argues that 'all in Christ' is qualified: only those who belong to Christ share in his resurrection life. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The NET note addresses subordinationism: the subordination is functional (mediatorial role), not ontological (divine nature). The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee identifies the Adam-Christ typology as the hermeneutical key. The resurrection is cosmic: the reversal of the Adamic curse. The 'last enemy' encompasses the entire complex of decay, corruption, and alienation from God. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton draws on Cullmann's 'already/not yet' framework. The tagma implies a structured sequence, not a simultaneous event. Christ's reign is a 'between the times' reality. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Paul constructs a positive theology centred on the Adam-Christ typology. Through Adam, death; through Christ, resurrection. Paul sketches an eschatological timeline using Psalm 110:1 and Psalm 8:6.

heb.paragraph: One of the most obscure expressions in the Pauline corpus, with over forty proposed interpretations. The most straightforward grammatical reading is vicarious baptism on behalf of the deceased. Paul does not endorse or condemn it; he cites it as evidence that even practitioners implicitly affirm resurrection.

refs.note: The Epicurean motto as the logical consequence of denying resurrection.
Baptism unites the believer with Christ's death and resurrection.

notes.note: Paul's argument is ad hominem: whatever 'baptism for the dead' means, it presupposes resurrection hope.

Paul's sufferings—including 'fighting wild beasts in Ephesus' (v. 32, likely metaphorical)—are evidence of resurrection faith. The Menander quotation warns against corrupting influence of resurrection-deniers.

Calvin surveys interpretations of v. 29 without dogmatic commitment. He insists on the practical application: those who deny resurrection inevitably drift toward moral laxity. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note surveys major options and leaves the question open, noting Paul's argument works regardless of which interpretation is correct. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee takes the grammatically straightforward reading: some Corinthians practised vicarious baptism. Paul uses it ad hominem without endorsing it. Without resurrection, the Christian life is absurd. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton surveys over forty interpretations and tentatively favours the view that 'the dead' refers to deceased believers whose lives motivated others to seek baptism. All interpretations are speculative. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: This section transitions from theological argument to practical implications. If the dead are not raised, both extraordinary practices (baptism for the dead) and daily apostolic sacrifice are pointless. The Isaiah 22:13 citation represents hedonism as the logical alternative to resurrection faith.

heb.paragraph: The verb speirō controls the seed analogy. A seed must die before producing a new plant; the plant differs radically from the seed while maintaining organic continuity. The resurrection body is related to the earthly body as a wheat stalk to a grain of wheat.

The adjective pair in v. 44 describes not composition but animating power: the present body is sustained by the natural life-principle (psychē); the resurrection body by the Holy Spirit (pneuma). The body remains a sōma—physical, real, tangible—but its power source is transformed.

refs.note: The scriptural basis for the Adam-Christ contrast. The 'living being' characterises the first creation; the 'life-giving spirit' characterises the new creation.
Christ 'will transform our lowly bodies so that they will be like his glorious body'—transformation, not replacement.

notes.note: The seed analogy: continuity with radical transformation. God gives each kind of seed 'its own body'—the resurrection body is divinely designed.

If God creates different kinds of flesh and different kinds of splendour, He can create a resurrection body. Four contrasts: perishable/imperishable, dishonour/glory, weakness/power, natural/spiritual.
The first Adam's body was 'natural'; the last Adam's is 'spiritual.' The order is deliberate: natural first, then spiritual. Believers now bear Adam's image but will bear Christ's.

Calvin insists the resurrection body is the same body, transformed—not a new creation from nothing. The 'natural/spiritual' contrast means the resurrection body is wholly animated by the Spirit, freed from mortality and weakness. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note emphasises 'spiritual' does not mean 'immaterial' but 'empowered by and oriented toward the Spirit.'
The NET note addresses the textual variant: 'we shall bear' (future indicative, better attested) vs. 'let us bear' (hortatory subjunctive).

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that sōma pneumatikon is a body radically transformed by the Spirit's power: physical, tangible, continuous with the present body, but freed from mortality. The Spirit who presently indwells believers will at the resurrection wholly transform their bodies.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton draws on philosophy of personal identity to argue that Paul navigates between mere resuscitation and abandonment of embodiment. The four-fold contrast series is structured chiastically, with natural/spiritual receiving the most development.

hist.context: Paul addresses 'With what kind of body?' (v. 35) through three analogies: the seed (vv. 36–38), diversity of flesh (vv. 39–41), and the contrast series (vv. 42–44). The Adam-Christ typology returns: the first Adam was a 'living being' (Gen 2:7 LXX); the last Adam is a 'life-giving spirit.'

heb.paragraph: The noun mystērion in v. 51 denotes a divine secret now revealed: 'We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed.' Living believers at the parousia will be instantaneously transformed. The term in Paul typically introduces a new eschatological disclosure (cf. Rom 11:25; Eph 3:3–6).

The noun nikos (victory) in vv. 54–57 clinches the triumph. Paul combines Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14 into a composite taunt-song against defeated death. The victory is already inaugurated in Christ's resurrection. The 'sting of death is sin' because sin gives death its power; God gives victory 'through our Lord Jesus Christ.'

refs.note: The eschatological vision: 'he will swallow up death forever.'
Reinterpreted by Paul as a victory song: death's sting is removed by Christ's resurrection.
Paul's earlier teaching about the parousia provides background for the 'mystery' of transformation.
'There will be no more death' echoes the Isaianic promise.

notes.note: The 'mystery': living believers will be transformed without dying, instantaneously, at 'the last trumpet.' The resurrected dead and transformed living share the same imperishable existence.

The clothing metaphor: the perishable 'clothes itself' with the imperishable—not shedding the body but transforming it. The victory song nullifies death. The theological chain: sting of death = sin, power of sin = law, victory = through Christ.

'Stand firm. Let nothing move you. Always give yourselves fully to the work of the Lord.' Because Christ is raised, nothing done for Him is wasted—a direct reversal of v. 14's 'useless' and v. 17's 'futile.'

Calvin sees the 'mystery' as addressing a genuine pastoral question. The victory song is among his most exultant treatments: death is not merely defeated but mocked. Verse 58's exhortation insists that resurrection hope is the foundation for tireless labour, not an escape from present responsibility.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses textual variants on v. 51 and favours 'We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed.'. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The NET note traces Paul's composite citation from Isaiah 25:8 LXX and Hosea 13:14 LXX, noting Paul's creative hermeneutics. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee regards this as the climax of Paul's entire argument. The 'mystery' addresses a gap in earlier teaching (1 Thess 4). Fee's Pentecostal eschatology emphasises the Spirit's role: the same Spirit who raised Jesus will transform believers' bodies. Verse 58 grounds all Christian ministry in resurrection certainty.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton draws on apocalyptic background: mystērion denotes a heavenly secret disclosed through revelation (cf. Dan 2:27–30). Paul reads Isaiah and Hosea through the hermeneutical lens of Christ's resurrection, transforming ambiguous texts into unambiguous victory declarations. Verse 58 exemplifies 'eschatological pragmatism': resurrection hope energises present labour.

hist.context: The climactic conclusion: a 'mystery' is disclosed—instantaneous transformation for the living at the parousia. Paul celebrates with Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14 as a taunt-song against death. Verse 58 connects resurrection hope to ethical perseverance: 'your labour in the Lord is not in vain.'

rows.label: vv.1–11
rows.text: The gospel of resurrection: creedal formula and appearances

rows.label: vv.12–19
rows.text: Reductio ad absurdum: seven consequences of denying resurrection

rows.label: vv.20–28
rows.text: Christ the firstfruits: Adam-Christ typology and eschatological timeline

rows.label: vv.29–34
rows.text: Practical implications: baptism for the dead and apostolic suffering

rows.label: vv.35–49
rows.text: The resurrection body: seed analogy, natural/spiritual contrast

rows.label: vv.50–58
rows.text: The mystery: transformation, victory over death, call to steadfastness

lit.note: Chapter 15 is the theological centrepiece of the letter, providing the most sustained treatment of resurrection in the NT.

themes.note: Faith dominates as the chapter concerns the content and object of Christian faith. Sovereignty scores highly as God's power over death is the central claim.

hebtext.note: The noun euangelion (gospel) frames the opening: Paul 'preached' it, they 'received' it, they 'stand' on it, they are 'saved' by it. The term here denotes a specific proclamation with defined content: Christ's death, burial, resurrection, and appearances (vv. 3–8). This is the earliest extant summary of the Christian kerygma, predating the written Gospels by at least a decade.

The verb egeirō in the perfect tense (egēgertai, 'has been raised,' v. 4) is theologically decisive. The perfect denotes a completed past action with ongoing present consequences: Christ was raised and remains raised. The passive implies divine agency: God raised Christ.
The noun kērygma (proclamation) in v. 14 denotes the message's content. If Christ has not been raised, the kērygma is 'useless' (kenon, empty)—a container without contents.
The adjective mataios (futile) in v. 17 describes faith without resurrection: it achieves nothing. The word echoes Ecclesiastes' mataiōtēs (vanity).

The noun aparchē (firstfruits) is an agricultural-cultic metaphor. Christ's resurrection is the firstfruits: not merely the first in a series but the guarantee that the full harvest will follow. The metaphor implies organic continuity.
The military noun tagma (order) arranges the resurrection in stages: Christ first, then believers at the parousia, then the consummation when Christ hands the kingdom to the Father.

One of the most obscure expressions in the Pauline corpus, with over forty proposed interpretations. The most straightforward grammatical reading is vicarious baptism on behalf of the deceased. Paul does not endorse or condemn it; he cites it as evidence that even practitioners implicitly affirm resurrection.

The verb speirō controls the seed analogy. A seed must die before producing a new plant; the plant differs radically from the seed while maintaining organic continuity. The resurrection body is related to the earthly body as a wheat stalk to a grain of wheat.

The adjective pair in v. 44 describes not composition but animating power: the present body is sustained by the natural life-principle (psychē); the resurrection body by the Holy Spirit (pneuma). The body remains a sōma—physical, real, tangible—but its power source is transformed.

The noun mystērion in v. 51 denotes a divine secret now revealed: 'We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed.' Living believers at the parousia will be instantaneously transformed. The term in Paul typically introduces a new eschatological disclosure (cf. Rom 11:25; Eph 3:3–6).

The noun nikos (victory) in vv. 54–57 clinches the triumph. Paul combines Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14 into a composite taunt-song against defeated death. The victory is already inaugurated in Christ's resurrection. The 'sting of death is sin' because sin gives death its power; God gives victory 'through our Lord Jesus Christ.'

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Cephas
ppl.text: ' The witness list is impressive: Cephas, the Twelve, over five hundred, James, all the apostles, and Paul…

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: ' The earliest written summary of the Christian gospel…

ppl.name: Adam
ppl.text: Paul constructs a positive theology centred on the Adam-Christ typology…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Resurrection of the Dead

translations.text: Now, brothers and sisters, I want to remind you of the gospel I preached to you, which you received and on which you have taken your stand.
Now I would remind you, brothers, of the gospel I preached to you, which you received, in which you stand,
After that, he appeared to more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at the same time, most of whom are still living, though some have fallen asleep.
Then he appeared to more than five hundred brothers at one time, most of whom are still alive, though some have fallen asleep.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Paul recalls the Corinthians to the foundational gospel. The conditional 'if you hold firmly' introduces pastoral urgency: some risk believing 'in vain.'"

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin treasures vv. 3–5 as the irreducible core of the gospel. The burial proves the death was physical; the appearances prove the resurrection was physical. The appeal to five hundred witnesses is a…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The NET note identifies vv. 3b–5 as a pre-Pauline creedal formula based on non-Pauline vocabulary, formulaic structure, and Paul's language of tradition reception."

debate: The NET note surveys major options and leaves the question open, noting Paul's argument works regardless of which interpretation is correct.…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Resurrection of the Dead within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: 'Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures' draws on the Suffering Servant passage.
The early church interpreted this as a prophecy of Christ's resurrection (cf. Acts 2:25–31).
The Gospel resurrection narratives expand the creedal statement.
Christ 'was raised to life for our justification'—resurrection is essential to salvation's application, not merely its confirmation.
Peter grounds Christian hope in the resurrection.
Death as a consequence of Adam's sin provides the negative background for the Adam-Christ contrast.

discourse.thesis: The resurrection of Christ is the factual basis and logical foundation of the entire gospel — without it faith is empty, sin is unforgiven, and the dead are lost; but since Christ rose, all in him will be made alive.

nodes.text: The gospel Paul preached and they received: Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, was buried, was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and appeared to Cephas, then the Twelve, then 500+ brothers, then James, then all the apostles, then to Paul himself. Whether Paul or the other apostles, this is what we preach and what you believed.

The logical consequence of resurrection denial: if there is no resurrection, Christ has not been raised; if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is empty and your faith is in vain; we are misrepresenting God; your sins are still unforgiven; those who fell asleep in Christ have perished. If only for this life we have hoped in Christ, we are most to be pitied (ἐλεεινότεροι πάντων ἀνθρώπων).

children.text: The Corinthian problem was not denial of Christ's resurrection but a denial that dead humans would be resurrected (v. 12 — 'some of you say there is no resurrection of the dead'). This Hellenistic view saw the body as prison; Paul insists the body's resurrection is essential to salvation, not incidental to it.

nodes.text: Christ has been raised — the firstfruits (ἀπαρχή) of those who have fallen asleep. As in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive. Each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ, then the end when he hands the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every ruler and authority and power. The last enemy to be destroyed is death.

children.text: Psalm 110:1 and Psalm 8:6 woven together: all enemies under Christ's feet, including death. When all things are subjected to the Son, then the Son himself will be subjected to the Father who put all things in subjection under him — that God may be all in all (πάντα ἐν πᾶσιν). The eschatological Telos: complete divine sovereignty, death abolished.

nodes.text: On the resurrection body: the 'how' question — a seed is sown and a different kind of body arises. There are earthly bodies and heavenly bodies, each with its own glory. So with the resurrection: sown perishable, raised imperishable; sown in dishonor, raised in glory; sown in weakness, raised in power; sown a natural body (ψυχικόν), raised a spiritual body (πνευματικόν). As we bore the image of the man of dust, we will also bear the image of the man of heaven.

children.text: The two-Adam typology: the first man Adam became a living being (Gen 2:7); the last Adam became a life-giving spirit. The spiritual was not first — the natural first, then the spiritual. The resurrection body is not the soul escaping the body but the body transformed by the Spirit into its final, glorious form.

nodes.text: The mystery: we shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed — in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. The dead will be raised imperishable and we shall be changed. When the perishable puts on the imperishable, death is swallowed up in victory (Isa 25:8). 'O Death, where is your sting? O Grave, where is your victory?' (Hos 13:14). Therefore, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord — because your labor in the Lord is not in vain.

children.text: The practical conclusion of the resurrection argument: 'your labor is not in vain.' The resurrection is not just cosmic eschatology — it is the ground of every present act of faithfulness. Work done in Christ has resurrection-permanence; it is not swept away with death.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 15 is the longest chapter in the letter and one of the most sustained theological arguments in the NT. It moves from historical evidence (vv. 1-11) to logical necessity (vv. 12-19) to cosmic scope (vv. 20-28) to practical implication (v. 58). The resurrection is not an add-on to Paul's theology but its foundation: without it, the cross achieves nothing and death wins.

coaching.tip: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile' (v. 17). Everything hangs on resurrection. Paul lists witnesses — Peter, the Twelve, 500 at once, James, all apostles, Paul himself (vv. 5-8). This is evidence, not myth. Christianity rises or falls with the empty tomb. No resurrection, no faith.

'O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?' (v. 55). Hosea 13:14 becomes triumphant taunt. Death's sting is sin; sin's power is law; but God gives victory through Christ (vv. 56-57). The enemy defeated, Paul concludes: 'Be steadfast, immovable... your labor is not in vain' (v. 58). Resurrection grounds perseverance.
